attributes.stem: A 71-year-old man undergoes routine endoscopy as part of follow-up in a Barrett's oesophagus surveillance program. He reports no new symptoms and the heartburn with which he initially presented is now well controlled with esomeprazole 20mg PO once daily. On this occasion, a 4 cm long Barrett's segment is confirmed. There is a small area of nodularity and four-quadrant biopsies are taken at 2 cm intervals. Two of the biopsies show high grade dysplasia and one other shows low grade dysplasia. There is no evidence of oesophagitis or a hiatus hernia.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
attributes.explanation:
The management approach to Barrett's oesophagus is influenced by patient factors and disease factors including the presence of dysplasia or visible mucosal irregularities. While there is some variation between guidelines, general recommendations are as follows:

  • Non-dysplastic Barrett's: endoscopic surveillance every 2-5 years depending on the length of the Barrett's segment

  • Indefinite for dysplasia: optimise medical reflux therapy and repeat endoscopy within 6 months

  • Low-grade dysplasia: either endoscopic eradication therapy or 6-monthly surveillance

  • High-grade dysplasia and early intramucosal adenocarcinoma: endoscopic eradication therapy, which may involve a combination of:

    • Ablative therapy (i.e. radiofrequency ablation) if no mucosal irregularities are present

    • Endoscopic resection (i.e. endoscopic mucosal resection or endoscopic submucosal dissection) of mucosal irregularities - this is both therapeutic and enables histological assessment of the resected specimen

    • If there is evidence of adenocarcinoma invasion beyond the submucosa, staging investigations and referral to an oncology centre

  • Visible mucosal irregularities should undergo endoscopic resection and histological examination (see high-grade dysplasia)

Given that this patient has Barrett's oesophagus with high grade dysplasia and nodularity, endoscopic resection is the most appropriate treatment. This technique allows for complete removal of the affected mucosa and provides tissue for detailed histological assessment, enabling the detection of adenocarcinoma not seen on biopsy. Endoscopic resection may be later followed by endoscopic radiofrequency ablation of the remaining Barrett's mucosa if no invasive adenocarcinoma is found. These interventions should ideally be performed in a specialised centre with expertise in Barrett's oesophagus management.

Repeat endoscopy in three months would be inadequate management given the high risk of progression to malignancy in high grade dysplasia.

Increasing esomeprazole dose would not address the underlying dysplastic changes and is not indicated given his reflux symptoms are controlled. While acid suppression is important in Barrett's oesophagus management, it will not reverse established dysplasia.

Endoscopic ultrasound would be appropriate if there was suspicion of invasive cancer to assess depth of invasion and lymph node involvement, but is not the initial management step for high grade dysplasia.
learningPoints: The presence of high grade dysplasia carries significant risk of progression to oesophageal adenocarcinoma.
High grade dysplasia in Barrett's oesophagus can be managed with endoscopic therapies
Endoscopic resection is preferred when mucosal irregularities are present
references: Shaheen NJ, Falk GW, Iyer PG, et al. Diagnosis and Management of Barrett's Esophagus: An Updated ACG Guideline. Am J Gastroenterol. 2022;117(4):559-587. Available at: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10259184/

Weusten BLAM, Bisschops R, Dinis-Ribeiro M, et al. Diagnosis and management of Barrett esophagus: European Society of Gastrointestinal Endoscopy (ESGE) Guideline. Endoscopy. 2023;55(12):1124-1146. Available at: https://www.thieme-connect.com/products/ejournals/abstract/10.1055/a-2176-2440

Whiteman DC, Kendall BJ. Barrett's oesophagus: epidemiology, diagnosis and clinical management. Med J Aust. 2016;205(7):317-324. Available at: https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/full/10.5694/mja16.00796
